trips.name: Classic Andalusia: Seville, Córdoba & Granada
trips.description: This is the grand tour of Moorish Andalusia — three cities that were among the most sophisticated in medieval Europe, each preserving its Islamic heritage in extraordinary completeness. Seville's Alcázar and the tower of La Giralda, Córdoba's Mezquita with its forest of arches, and Granada's Alhambra palace complex constitute the most concentrated sequence of great monuments in Spain. Flamenco, tapas, and sherry are woven through every stop.
trips.cover_search_query: Alhambra Granada palace Moorish Andalusia Spain

trips.name: Ronda & the White Villages
trips.description: The Pueblos Blancos — white villages — of the Cádiz and Málaga sierras are among the most photogenic villages in Europe: cubic whitewashed buildings cascading down steep hillsides above olive groves and wild mountains. Ronda's Tajo gorge splits the town in two across a 120-metre chasm, and the 18th-century bullring is the oldest in Spain. This is Andalusia unmediated by mass tourism.
trips.cover_search_query: Ronda bridge El Tajo gorge Andalusia Spain

trips.name: Costa de la Luz & Doñana
trips.description: Spain's most unspoiled Atlantic coastline faces the open ocean with long, wind-swept beaches, kitesurfing meccas, and fishing villages where the tuna catch is still celebrated as it was in ancient Carthage. Doñana National Park — Europe's most important wetland — shelters the last Iberian lynx and hundreds of thousands of wintering waterbirds in a landscape where the Atlantic dunes meet the Guadalquivir marshes.

stops.name: El Puerto de Santa María, Spain
stops.label: El Puerto de Santa María

highlights: Doñana National Park lynx and birds
trips.cover_search_query: Doñana National Park wetlands Huelva Andalusia Spain

trips.name: Almería Desert Coast
trips.description: The province of Almería is Spain's surprise — the driest corner of Europe has Europe's only desert (the Tabernas, used for every spaghetti western Leone ever made), turquoise volcanic coves at the Cabo de Gata natural park, and the former mining ghost towns of the Sierra Minera. This is the Spain that exists before mass tourism, where the light is fierce and the landscapes biblical.
trips.cover_search_query: Cabo de Gata volcanic coves Almería Andalusia Spain

trips.name: Jaén Olive Kingdom & Úbeda-Baeza
trips.description: The province of Jaén produces more olive oil than Greece — an ocean of silver-green olive trees covering every hill for 200 kilometres in the most productive agricultural landscape in Spain. Rising from this sea are the twin Renaissance cities of Úbeda and Baeza, both UNESCO World Heritage Sites, where 16th-century nobles competed to build the grandest palaces and churches in a display of Renaissance patronage equalling anything in Italy.
trips.cover_search_query: Úbeda Renaissance plaza Jaén Andalusia Spain

trips.name: Las Alpujarras & Sierra Nevada
trips.description: The Alpujarras is the hidden southern slope of the Sierra Nevada — a landscape of terraced valleys, acequia irrigation channels, and Berber-style flat-roofed villages clinging to gorges at 1,500 metres that was the last redoubt of the Moors expelled from Granada. Capileira, Bubión, and Pampaneira are three villages in the Poqueira gorge that look as though they belong in Morocco. Above them, Sierra Nevada's 3,479-metre Mulhacén is the highest peak in mainland Spain.
trips.cover_search_query: Las Alpujarras mountain villages Sierra Nevada Spain

trips.name: Córdoba Province
trips.description: The countryside around Córdoba is a world away from the coastal tourist circuit — limestone gorges of the Subbética, the wine town of Montilla, and the chalk-white hilltop villages of the Campiña that look exactly as they did in the paintings of Velázquez and Zurbarán. Medina Azahara, the extraordinary ruined palace city of the Umayyad caliphs, is one of the most impressive archaeological sites in Spain.
trips.cover_search_query: Medina Azahara Umayyad palace ruins Córdoba Spain

trips.name: Huelva & Columbus Country
trips.description: The province of Huelva has one of the most dramatic backstories in world history — it was from Palos de la Frontera that Columbus sailed in August 1492 to discover the Americas. La Rábida monastery was where Columbus found monastic support for his plan; the Columbian Monuments at Punta del Sebo mark the confluence of the Odiel and Tinto rivers. Today the province is also famous for the world's finest jamón ibérico, raised on acorns in the dehesa forests of Jabugo.
highlights: Palos de la Frontera Columbus departure
trips.cover_search_query: La Rábida monastery Columbus Huelva Andalusia Spain

trips.name: Málaga & Axarquía
trips.description: Málaga has reinvented itself as Spain's most exciting cultural city — Picasso's birthplace now has 30 world-class museums in a dazzling revitalised centre, and the tapas bars of the Malagueta and Soho neighbourhoods are among the best in Andalusia. The Axarquía mountains east of Málaga are a landscape of Moorish sugar-farming terraces, raisin-grape vineyards, and cliff-edge white villages connected by ancient mule tracks.
highlights: Nerja Balcón de Europa sea view
trips.cover_search_query: Málaga city cathedral port Andalusia Spain

trips.name: Barcelona & Hinterland
trips.description: Barcelona is simultaneously one of the world's great cities and the gateway to the Catalan hinterland of Modernisme villas, medieval monasteries, and wine-producing plateaus. Gaudí's unfinished Sagrada Família rises above a city already crowded with architectural miracles; within an hour's drive lie the monastery of Montserrat on its extraordinary serrated rock, the medieval walled town of Vic, and the Penedès vineyards producing Spain's best cava.
trips.cover_search_query: Sagrada Família Barcelona Gaudí Catalonia Spain

trips.name: Costa Brava & Dalí Country
trips.description: The Costa Brava between Blanes and the French border is the most rugged and beautiful coast in Spain — pine-covered headlands dropping to crystal-clear coves, medieval fishing villages like Calella de Palafrugell, and the Greek-Roman ruins of Empúries beside a horseshoe bay. Salvador Dalí lived and worked in Cap de Creus — the easternmost point of the Iberian peninsula — and his trio of museums (Figueres, Gala Dalí, and the Portlligat house) trace a surrealist biography.
trips.cover_search_query: Cadaqués white village Costa Brava Catalonia Spain

trips.name: Catalan Pyrenees
trips.description: The Catalan Pyrenees rise abruptly from the Mediterranean foothills to peaks exceeding 3,000 metres, sheltering Romanesque churches of extraordinary refinement in valleys that were the cradle of medieval Catalonia. The National Park of Aigüestortes i Estany de Sant Maurici is Spain's finest mountain park — a wilderness of alpine lakes, granite cirques, and glacial valleys that is the Pyrenean equivalent of the Alps.
trips.cover_search_query: Aigüestortes alpine lake granite Pyrenees Catalonia Spain

trips.name: Tarragona & the Ebre Delta
trips.description: Tarragona was the capital of Roman Hispania — its extraordinary circuit of Roman walls, amphitheatre overlooking the sea, circus, and forum make it one of the most important Roman cities in the western Mediterranean. The Ebre Delta south of the city is an extraordinary flat wetland of rice paddies and lagoons where more than 300 bird species gather — a world utterly unlike the Spanish coast either side of it.
trips.cover_search_query: Tarragona Roman amphitheatre sea Catalonia Spain

trips.name: Girona Medieval City
trips.description: Girona is one of the best-preserved medieval cities in Europe — a compact old town of Jewish quarter alleyways, Romanesque cathedral, and Arab baths rising above the Onyar river whose colourful house façades are reflected in the water below. The surrounding county of Gironès has the volcanic landscape of La Garrotxa — a surreal terrain of perfectly round volcanic cones covered in beech forest with lava flows underfoot.
trips.cover_search_query: Girona Onyar river coloured houses medieval Catalonia Spain

trips.name: Penedès & Priorat Wine Roads
trips.description: Catalonia's two great wine regions offer a study in contrasts: the broad Penedès plateau produces most of Spain's cava from Macabeu, Parellada, and Xarel·lo grapes in a landscape of cellar-dotted countryside; while the steep, terraced Priorat (DOCa alongside Rioja) grows its intense Garnacha and Cariñena reds on black slate and mica soil that gives the wines a mineral complexity unique in Spain.
trips.cover_search_query: Priorat vineyards terraces Catalonia Spain

trips.name: Montserrat & Central Catalonia
trips.description: The central inland area of Catalonia, away from the coast, reveals the region's medieval heartland — Berguedà's pre-Pyrenean foothills, the extraordinary serrated mountain of Montserrat rising from the plain, and the medieval textile towns of the Llobregat valley. Cardona's rock-salt mountain has been mined since the Bronze Age and includes an extraordinary medieval castle that is now a parador; the Romanesque churches of the Berguedà are among the finest in Catalonia.
trips.cover_search_query: Montserrat monastery serrated mountain Catalonia Spain

trips.name: Salamanca, Zamora & the Duero
trips.description: Salamanca is Spain's greatest university city — a golden sandstone city of Baroque plateresque façades, Renaissance colleges, and one of Europe's oldest universities (1218) that still fills the Plaza Mayor with students in the evening. Zamora has the finest ensemble of Romanesque churches in Spain, concentrated in a small old town above the Duero river, whose valley south of the city gives way to the Arribes del Duero gorges — a dramatic canyon marking the Portuguese border.
trips.cover_search_query: Salamanca Plaza Mayor golden sandstone Spain

trips.name: Burgos & the Camino de Santiago
trips.description: The Camino Francés crosses the meseta of Castile and León from the mountains above Burgos to the mountains above León — 200 kilometres of the most famous walking route in the world, passing through wheat plains so vast the horizon is unbroken for 40 kilometres. Burgos Cathedral is one of the three great Gothic cathedrals in Spain; the road passes the ruined monastery of San Juan de Ortega and the mediaeval arch at Santo Domingo de la Calzada where a live cockerel has been kept for 500 years.

stops.name: Santo Domingo de la Calzada, Spain
stops.label: Santo Domingo de la Calzada

trips.cover_search_query: Burgos Cathedral Gothic spires Castile Spain

trips.name: Ávila, Segovia & the Guadarrama
trips.description: The mountain wall of the Guadarrama separates Madrid from the high Castilian plateau — and on the far side sit two of Spain's most complete medieval cities. Ávila is entirely enclosed in its Romanesque walls (2.5 kilometres, perfectly intact) and is the birthplace of Santa Teresa; Segovia has a Roman aqueduct still standing on its 166 stone arches and a fairy-tale Alcázar that directly inspired Walt Disney's Cinderella Castle.

stops.name: La Granja de San Ildefonso, Spain
stops.label: La Granja

trips.cover_search_query: Segovia Roman aqueduct Castile Spain

trips.name: Soria & the Duero Canyon
trips.description: Soria is the least-visited provincial capital in Spain — a quiet city of Romanesque churches, medieval towers, and the poetry of Antonio Machado who taught there in the early 20th century and gave it immortality in verse. The Cañón del Río Lobos and the Duero gorges cut through limestone plateaus in some of the most dramatic, emptiest landscapes in Spain, where griffon vultures wheel above pine forests.
trips.cover_search_query: Cañón del Río Lobos limestone gorge Soria Spain

trips.name: Valladolid & Ribera del Duero Wine
trips.description: The Ribera del Duero is Spain's second great Tempranillo wine region — an arid plateau at 800 metres producing structured, powerful reds (Vega Sicilia, Pesquera, Pingus) from extreme continental conditions. The road from Valladolid through Peñafiel and Aranda de Duero follows the river through a landscape of castle-topped hills, medieval stone villages, and bodegas carved into the hillsides.
trips.cover_search_query: Peñafiel castle Ribera del Duero vineyard Spain

trips.name: El Bierzo & Maragatería
trips.description: El Bierzo is the transitional land where the Castilian plateau gives way to the Galician mountains — a sheltered depression of cherry orchards, slate-roofed villages, and the unique Mencía grape producing reds of extraordinary mineral character. The Camino de Santiago crosses it via the medieval village of Molinaseca and the imposing castle of Ponferrada — a Templar stronghold that protected pilgrims en route to Santiago.
highlights: Las Médulas Roman gold mine UNESCO
trips.cover_search_query: Las Médulas Roman gold mine El Bierzo León Spain

trips.name: San Sebastián & Gipuzkoa
trips.description: San Sebastián (Donostia) is the gastronomic capital of Europe — more Michelin stars per capita than anywhere else, a pintxos bar culture in the Parte Vieja that operates with the seriousness of a culinary ceremony, and a perfect shell beach enclosed by two headlands that makes it the most elegantly situated city in Spain. The Gipuzkoa coast behind it has fishing villages, dramatic clifftops at Zumaia's flysch strata, and the Sanctuary of Loyola.
trips.cover_search_query: San Sebastián La Concha beach Basque Country Spain

trips.name: Bilbao & the Basque Interior
trips.description: Frank Gehry's Guggenheim Bilbao is the defining building of the 1990s — a titanium-clad sculpture that single-handedly transformed a post-industrial port into an international cultural destination. The Basque interior behind it is a landscape of green valleys, hilltop hermitages, and prehistoric dolmens that predates the Basque language itself. The Orduña mountain pass and the beech forests of Gorbeia Natural Park are utterly removed from the Guggenheim's glamour.
trips.cover_search_query: Guggenheim Bilbao titanium Basque Country Spain

trips.name: Pamplona & Navarre
trips.description: Navarre is the gateway to Spain — the kingdom through which pilgrims, conquerors, and traders entered the peninsula via the Pyrenees for millennia. Pamplona is defined for the world by Hemingway and the Running of the Bulls, but its walled city and medieval street grid are extraordinary regardless of the festival. The Navarrese Pyrenees shelter the unique Valle del Roncal and the Romanesque monastery of Leyre perched above an inland sea.
highlights: Pamplona city walls and old town
trips.cover_search_query: Pamplona city walls Navarre Spain

trips.name: La Rioja Wine Road
trips.description: La Rioja is Spain's most famous wine region — a red-soiled valley sheltered by the Sierra de Cantabria mountains where Tempranillo has been aged in American oak since the 19th century. The wine road between Logroño and Haro passes celebrated bodegas that have become architectural commissions for Calatrava, Hadid, Gehry, and Nouvel. The monastery of San Millán de la Cogolla, where the first written words in Castilian Spanish were found, is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

stops.name: San Millán de la Cogolla, Spain
stops.label: San Millán de la Cogolla

highlights: Marqués de Riscal Frank Gehry winery
San Millán de la Cogolla UNESCO
trips.cover_search_query: Marqués de Riscal Gehry winery La Rioja Spain

trips.name: Bardenas Reales Badlands
trips.description: The Bardenas Reales is Spain's most otherworldly landscape — a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve of eroded clay plateaus, mushroom-shaped earth pillars, and desert ravines that look like they belong in Arizona but sit barely 50 kilometres from Pamplona. The plateau is dissected by dry riverbeds, white clay cliffs, and the extraordinary Castil de Tierra pinnacle that rises 50 metres from the plain. This is the location that has doubled for every Middle Eastern and North African landscape in Spanish cinema.
trips.cover_search_query: Bardenas Reales badlands desert Navarre Spain

trips.name: Camino's Final Stretch to Santiago
trips.description: The last 100 kilometres of the Camino Francés from O Cebreiro to Santiago de Compostela pass through Galicia's oakwood-covered hills, rural parishes, and medieval stone bridges in a final crescendo that leads to the extraordinary Romanesque-Baroque cathedral on the Plaza del Obradoiro — one of the great architectural compositions in Europe. The journey takes pilgrims through the most intensely green and rain-washed landscape in Spain.
highlights: O Cebreiro mountain pass stone church
trips.cover_search_query: Santiago de Compostela Cathedral Galicia Spain

trips.name: Rías Baixas & Albariño
trips.description: The Rías Baixas are the drowned river valleys of southwest Galicia — sheltered sea inlets where mussel and oyster cultivation has produced the most productive shellfish grounds in Europe. The inland valleys above grow Albariño — Galicia's great white wine grape — in a system of granite pergolas called emparrado that holds the vines high above the Atlantic moisture. Pontevedra's medieval stone centre is one of Spain's most complete old towns.
trips.cover_search_query: Rías Baixas estuary mussel farms Galicia Spain

trips.name: Ribeira Sacra Canyon Vineyards
trips.description: The Ribeira Sacra is one of the most dramatic wine landscapes in Europe — Mencía grapes grown on terraces of schist rock clinging to the 600-metre walls of the Sil and Miño river canyons. Boat trips from Orense through the Cañón do Sil reveal the vineyards from below in a scene of extraordinary wildness. Romanesque monasteries perch on the canyon ridges above, and the catamaran from Os Peares is the finest way to see the whole extraordinary landscape.
highlights: Parada de Sil monastery canyon view
trips.cover_search_query: Ribeira Sacra canyon vineyard terrace Sil Galicia Spain

trips.name: Wild Atlantic Galician Coast
trips.description: The Costa da Morte — 'Coast of Death' — is Galicia's dramatic northwestern tip where the Atlantic hurls itself at sheer granite cliffs with a ferocity that has wrecked thousands of ships. The lighthouse at Cape Finisterre was long believed to be the western edge of the known world, and pilgrims still walk here after Santiago to stand at the end of the earth. Malpica, Fisterra, and Muxía are fishing villages of real character at the margin of the world.
trips.cover_search_query: Cabo Fisterra lighthouse Atlantic Galicia Spain

trips.name: Picos de Europa
trips.description: The Picos de Europa is Spain's most dramatic mountain range — limestone towers rising to 2,648 metres just 15 kilometres from the Cantabrian Sea, creating a landscape of impossible vertical relief visible from the beaches below. The Cares Gorge (Ruta del Cares) cuts 12 kilometres through the mountains on a path carved from the cliff face; Covadonga is the spiritual birthplace of Spain, where the Reconquest began in 722 AD.
trips.cover_search_query: Picos de Europa limestone peaks Asturias Cantabria Spain

trips.name: Asturian Cider Coast
trips.description: Asturias is where the Celts went when they fell off the edge of Europe — a green, rain-soaked, orchard-covered Atlantic fringe where cider (sidra) is poured from shoulder height into the glass and the cultural identity owes more to Ireland than to Castile. The Jurassic Coast fossils at the Playa de la Ballota, the pre-Romanesque churches of Oviedo and the Naranco hill, and the fishing villages of the cliffed coast round out a region utterly unlike the Spain of tourist imagination.
trips.cover_search_query: Asturias green coast sidra cider fishing village Spain

trips.name: Cantabria & Altamira
trips.description: Cantabria punches well above its size — the world's greatest prehistoric cave art (Altamira), a spectacular coastal range of cliff villages and limestone skerries at the Liencres dunes, and the medieval old town of Santillana del Mar, which Sartre called 'the most beautiful village in Spain'. The Museo de Cantabria in Santander has one of Spain's finest regional collections.

stops.name: San Vicente de la Barquera, Spain
stops.label: San Vicente de la Barquera

highlights: San Vicente de la Barquera harbour
trips.cover_search_query: Santillana del Mar medieval village Cantabria Spain

trips.name: Liébana Valley & Fuente Dé
trips.description: The Liébana valley is a hidden world enclosed by the Picos de Europa on three sides — a microclimate so sheltered from the Atlantic that grapes, olives, and citrus trees grow at 300 metres altitude while snow sits on the peaks above year-round. The Fuente Dé cable car ascends 800 metres of sheer cliff in four minutes to a high plateau of ibex and chamois; the Monastery of Santo Toribio claims to hold the largest fragment of the True Cross in the world.
highlights: Fuente Dé cable car cliff ascent
trips.cover_search_query: Fuente Dé cable car Picos de Europa Cantabria Spain

trips.name: Somiedo & the Asturian Reserve
trips.description: The Parque Natural de Somiedo is one of Spain's highest-designated nature reserves — a landscape of glacial lakes, bracken moors, and brown bear territory that preserves an ancient transhumance culture of shepherd cabins (brañas) that have barely changed since medieval times. The Leitariegos pass and the Degaña valley give access to one of the few remaining wild brown bear populations in Spain.
trips.cover_search_query: Somiedo lake mountain brañas Asturias Spain

trips.name: Ordesa & Monte Perdido
trips.description: The Parque Nacional de Ordesa y Monte Perdido is Spain's oldest and grandest national park — a UNESCO World Heritage Site of glacier-carved canyons, vertical walls rising 1,500 metres, and waterfalls thundering from hanging valleys. The Ordesa Canyon walk through the valley floor is one of the finest half-day walks in Europe; the Anisclo gorge and the Escuaín canyon offer wilder variants for those who go further.
trips.cover_search_query: Ordesa Canyon Monte Perdido Pyrenees Aragon Spain

trips.name: Teruel & the Mudéjar Route
trips.description: Teruel is the Spanish city that most tourists have never heard of — a UNESCO-recognised city of Mudéjar towers where Christian builders adopted Islamic decorative techniques to produce a uniquely Spanish hybrid architecture of tile-clad brick minarets. The Albarracín walled village above the Guadalaviar gorge is consistently voted Spain's most beautiful village; the Maestrazgo plateau beyond offers empty highland landscapes of medieval mastery.
trips.cover_search_query: Albarracín pink walled medieval village Teruel Spain

trips.name: Benasque & the Aragonese Pyrenees
trips.description: The Benasque valley contains the highest terrain in the Pyrenees — Aneto at 3,404 metres is the range's highest peak, rising above the Maladeta massif's glaciers in the Posets-Maladeta Nature Reserve. The valley road through Anciles and Cerler to the Portillon pass offers the most dramatic driving in the Aragonese Pyrenees, and the village of Benasque itself has become one of Spain's most popular mountain resort towns without losing its stone-built Pyrenean character.
trips.cover_search_query: Benasque Pyrenees valley mountains Aragon Spain

trips.name: Jaca & the Romanesque Route
trips.description: Jaca was the first capital of the Kingdom of Aragon and its cathedral is the oldest Romanesque church in Spain, built in the 11th century as a statement of Christian power at the foot of the Pyrenean passes. The Serrablo route through the Gállego valley connects a series of pre-Romanesque and Romanesque churches of extraordinary refinement in tiny mountain villages; the monastery of San Juan de la Peña clings to an overhanging cliff in a setting of theatrical impossibility.

stops.name: San Juan de la Peña, Spain
stops.label: San Juan de la Peña

highlights: San Juan de la Peña overhanging cliff monastery
Sos del Rey Católico medieval village
trips.cover_search_query: San Juan de la Peña cliff monastery Aragon Spain

trips.name: Valencia City & Coast
trips.description: Valencia is Spain's third city and its most underrated — a place of spectacular architectural contrasts where the futuristic City of Arts and Sciences sits beside an ornate Gothic silk exchange, and where paella was invented in the rice fields of the Albufera lagoon just south of the city. The Costa del Azahar north of Valencia has the best undiscovered beaches in the region, backed by orange grove flatlands filling the air with blossom each February.
highlights: Valencia City of Arts and Sciences
trips.cover_search_query: Valencia City of Arts and Sciences Spain

trips.name: Costa Blanca
trips.description: The Costa Blanca between Dénia and Alicante has two personalities: the northern stretch around Jávea and Altea has preserved the character of whitewashed fishing villages on dramatic capes, while the southern half from Benidorm to Alicante offers Europe's most developed resort strip in every sense. Alicante's castle of Santa Bárbara above the port is one of the largest medieval fortresses in Spain, and Elche's UNESCO-listed palm grove is one of the most remarkable landscapes in Europe.
trips.cover_search_query: Jávea cape Costa Blanca Alicante Spain

trips.name: Valencia Inland & Morella
trips.description: The inland provinces of Valencia contain some of the most dramatic limestone landscapes in Spain — the Maestrazgo plateau of medieval villages, the gorges of the Júcar river, and the extraordinary walled hilltop city of Morella crowning a 1,000-metre rock with a ruined castle above that can be seen from 30 kilometres in every direction. Requena is the gateway to the Utiel-Requena wine denomination's earthy Bobal reds.
trips.cover_search_query: Morella walled medieval hilltop fortress Valencia Spain

trips.name: Murcia & Cartagena
trips.description: The Mar Menor — the largest saltwater lagoon in Europe — and the craggy limestone coast of the Costa Cálida frame Murcia's coastal landscape. Cartagena has one of the best-presented Roman heritage trails in Spain — the Roman Theatre, Punic wall, and Naval Museum combine in an outstanding archaeological experience — while Lorca's Baroque heritage is the most concentrated in the region.

stops.name: La Manga del Mar Menor, Spain
stops.label: La Manga del Mar Menor

trips.cover_search_query: Cartagena Roman theatre ancient port Murcia Spain

trips.name: Madrid & Royal Castile
trips.description: Madrid's three world-class art museums — the Prado, Reina Sofía, and Thyssen-Bornemisza — form the 'Golden Triangle of Art', making the capital one of the world's great museum cities. The royal palaces around Madrid are equally extraordinary: the Escorial is Philip II's brooding granite megamonastery in the Guadarrama mountains; Aranjuez's Versailles-influenced gardens run along the Tagus in a baroque spectacle of water and topiary.
trips.cover_search_query: Madrid Prado Museum Gran Via Spain

trips.name: Cuenca & Don Quixote's La Mancha
trips.description: Cuenca hangs at the edge of a gorge in one of the most improbable urban settings in Spain — the medieval city's 'hanging houses' (Casas Colgadas) project over a 60-metre drop to the Huécar river below. La Mancha's immense wheat plain extends south, where the windmills of Consuegra and Campo de Criptana stand on ridges exactly as Cervantes described — giants, according to Don Quixote — and the saffron fields around Consuegra are harvested in October.
trips.cover_search_query: Cuenca hanging houses gorge Castilla-La Mancha Spain

trips.name: Guadalajara & the Alcarria
trips.description: The Alcarria plateau between Guadalajara and Cuenca is the landscape that Camilo José Cela walked in 1946 to write one of the great Spanish travel books — a forgotten region of lavender fields, Moorish fortresses, beehive villages, and the extraordinary ducal palace of Pastrana. Sigüenza's cathedral is one of the finest Romanesque-Gothic transitional buildings in Spain, and the town around it is a perfectly preserved Castilian medieval city.
trips.cover_search_query: Sigüenza medieval cathedral Guadalajara Spain

trips.name: Mérida & Roman Extremadura
trips.description: Mérida is the most complete Roman city in Spain — the provincial capital of Lusitania retains its theatre (where performances are still staged in summer), amphitheatre, circus, aqueduct, and a collection of Roman bridges and temples of extraordinary richness now in a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Extremadura's vast dehesa landscape of cork and holm oaks spreads in every direction, a landscape that has sustained the great Ibérico pig for centuries.
trips.cover_search_query: Mérida Roman theatre amphitheatre Spain

trips.name: Monfragüe & Plasencia
trips.description: Monfragüe National Park is the finest raptor-watching site in Europe — a gorge in the Tagus river where black vultures, Spanish imperial eagles, and black storks nest on quartzite crags in numbers that are impossible to believe until you see them yourself. Plasencia is a lovely provincial city with a Gothic-Renaissance cathedral double (an old and new cathedral attached) and a Wednesday market on the Plaza Mayor that has operated since the 12th century.
trips.cover_search_query: Monfragüe raptor black vulture Extremadura Spain

trips.name: Cáceres & Guadalupe Monastery
trips.description: Cáceres is one of the finest intact medieval cities in Europe — its UNESCO-listed old town of noble towers and palaces was the destination for the conquistadors' wealth returning from the Americas, which funded an architectural legacy that fills the walled hilltop with Renaissance and late Gothic buildings in volcanic granite. The Royal Monastery of Guadalupe in the Villuercas mountains preserves the patroness of all Latin America and a sacristy full of Zurbarán masterpieces.
trips.cover_search_query: Cáceres medieval old town UNESCO Extremadura Spain

trips.name: Mallorca: Tramuntana Mountains
trips.description: The Serra de Tramuntana runs the entire northwestern spine of Mallorca — a UNESCO World Heritage Site of terraced olive groves, mountain villages, and dramatic sea cliffs that drops 1,000 metres to the Mediterranean in a single escarpment. The road from Andratx to Pollença is one of the great coastal mountain drives of the Mediterranean; Deià's stone village, where Robert Graves lived and wrote, is among the most beautiful in Spain.
trips.cover_search_query: Mallorca Tramuntana mountains coast village Spain

trips.name: Mallorca East: Caves & Coves
trips.description: The eastern coast of Mallorca has a completely different character from the mountainous northwest — a landscape of limestone coves (calas) with impossibly turquoise water, the extraordinary Coves del Drac caverns with their underground lake, and the medieval hilltop village of Artà with its fortified church above a Talayotic prehistoric site. The narrow roads between calas on the southeast coast are some of the most scenic driving in the Balearics.
trips.cover_search_query: Coves del Drac underground lake Mallorca Spain

trips.name: Menorca Prehistoric Circuit
trips.description: Menorca is the most complete prehistoric island in the Mediterranean — a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve whose Bronze Age Talayotic culture left over 1,500 megalithic monuments including the mysterious taules (T-shaped stone slabs up to 4 metres high) and navetes (boat-shaped tombs) scattered across the island in extraordinary density. The unspoiled northern coast of red sandstone cliffs provides a dramatic contrast to the turquoise southern calas.
trips.cover_search_query: Menorca Talayotic taula prehistoric megalith Spain

trips.name: Ibiza & Formentera
trips.description: Ibiza's UNESCO-listed old city (Dalt Vila) rises above the harbour in a ring of Renaissance walls built by Emperor Charles V — a perfectly preserved medieval citadel that exists in remarkable contrast to the world's most famous club island below. The ferry to Formentera crosses in 30 minutes to an island of flat terraces, salt pans, and the clearest shallow water in the western Mediterranean — the sea here runs from transparent turquoise to deep azure in gradations that photographers never quite capture.
highlights: Ibiza sunset at Café del Mar
trips.cover_search_query: Ibiza Dalt Vila old town harbour Spain

trips.name: Tenerife & Teide
trips.description: Tenerife is dominated by Teide — at 3,718 metres the highest peak in Spain and the third-largest volcano on earth from its sea-floor base. The Teide National Park is a UNESCO World Heritage Site of extraordinary other-worldly lava landscapes — calderas, lava tubes, and multicoloured pumice fields that make the summit seem removed from Europe entirely. The north of the island around La Orotava has banana groves, colonial architecture, and the original Dragon Trees.
trips.cover_search_query: Teide volcano Tenerife Canary Islands Spain

trips.name: Gran Canaria
trips.description: Gran Canaria packs a continent's worth of landscapes into a circular island of 1,500 square kilometres — the Saharan dunes of Maspalomas in the south, the pine forests and ancient rock engravings of the Roque Nublo plateau, the banana plantations of the west coast, and the sandstone cave dwellings of the Guanche people in the Barranco de Guayadeque. Las Palmas is one of Spain's most underrated cities, with an excellent old town and the house where Columbus stopped en route to America.

stops.name: Las Palmas de Gran Canaria, Spain
stops.label: Las Palmas

trips.cover_search_query: Maspalomas sand dunes Gran Canaria Spain

trips.name: Lanzarote & César Manrique
trips.description: Lanzarote is unique in the Canaries — a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve where the artist César Manrique transformed the volcanic landscape into an extraordinary integrated artwork. His Jameos del Agua, Cueva de los Verdes, and Jardín de Cactus are seamlessly blended into the black lava terrain. The Timanfaya National Park is an active volcanic field where the magma is still only 15 metres below the surface, heating the ground to 600°C.
highlights: Cueva de los Verdes lava tube
trips.cover_search_query: Timanfaya volcanic landscape Lanzarote Canary Islands Spain

trips.name: Fuerteventura
trips.description: Fuerteventura is the oldest and most African of the Canary Islands — a desert landscape of Saharan winds, ancient eroded volcanic mountains, and 150 kilometres of beach that constitute the finest windsurfing and kitesurfing conditions in Europe. The Jandía peninsula in the south has beaches of powdered white shell sand; the Betancuria historic village in the volcanic interior was the island's capital for three centuries.
trips.cover_search_query: Fuerteventura white sand beach dunes Canary Islands Spain

trips.name: La Palma & La Gomera
trips.description: La Palma is the greenest and most dramatic of the Canaries — its Caldera de Taburiente is the world's largest erosion caldera, 8 kilometres across and 1,500 metres deep, now a national park of ancient Canarian pine forest. La Gomera's Garajonay National Park is a primordial laurel forest so thick and misty it is a UNESCO World Heritage Site; the Silbo Gomero whistle language (also UNESCO-listed) echoes between the island's deep barrancos.

stops.name: Santa Cruz de la Palma, Spain
stops.label: Santa Cruz de la Palma

stops.name: San Sebastián de la Gomera, Spain
stops.label: San Sebastián de la Gomera

trips.cover_search_query: Caldera de Taburiente La Palma Canary Islands Spain

trips.name: Green Spain: End-to-End Cantabrian Coast
trips.description: The full Cantabrian coast from the Basque Country to Galicia is one of Europe's great road trips — a green, rain-soaked, Atlantic-facing arc of fishing villages, cliff scenery, cider bars, and pilgrimage routes that feels entirely different from Mediterranean Spain. Crossing from the surf beaches of the Basque Country through Asturias's pre-Romanesque churches and the Picos de Europa to the Celtic harbours of Galicia, this is a journey through the 'other Spain' that most visitors never find.
trips.cover_search_query: Cantabrian coast green Spain Atlantic cliffs fishing village
